text.title: Procurement Process: Make-or-Buy, SOW, Contract Types
text.summary: Core procurement workflow: decide make vs buy, define scope via SOW, choose contract type, source vendors, and manage ongoing performance.

section.title: Make-or-Buy Decision

rule.statement: Decide make-vs-buy before writing any SOW. Evaluate cost, control, speed, expertise, and risk transfer explicitly.
rule.rationale: Skipping this step leads to outsourcing work that should be internal (losing IP) or building internally when a vendor is cheaper and faster.

section.p: Key factors: fixed vs variable cost, control level, access to specialist expertise, risk ownership, and long-term switching cost. Switching cost is routinely underestimated — factor in knowledge transfer, vendor lock-in, and transition time.

section.title: Statement of Work

rule.statement: Every external engagement requires a SOW with: scope (in and out), numbered deliverables, acceptance criteria per deliverable, milestone dates, and payment tied to acceptance not activity.
rule.rationale: Vague SOWs are the top cause of scope disputes. Acceptance-linked payment aligns vendor incentives with buyer outcomes.

section.reference: SOW skeleton with all required sections

example.note: SOW says "develop the mobile app to a high standard" — no deliverables listed, no acceptance criteria, payment on delivery date.
SOW lists D1 (design mockups, accepted when stakeholder sign-off), D2 (working app, accepted when App Store approval + 95% crash-free rate), payment 25% / 50% / 25% at milestone acceptance.

rule.statement: Choose FFP when scope is at least 80% defined; T&M with a NOT-TO-EXCEED cap when scope is evolving; CPFF only for research with inherently unknowable cost.
rule.rationale: FFP without defined scope incentivises vendors to minimise effort. Uncapped T&M transfers all cost risk to the buyer. CPFF for routine work is fiscally irresponsible.

rule.statement: Any T&M contract must include a weekly burn report and a hard cap. Uncapped T&M is open-ended risk.
rule.rationale: Without a cap, T&M scope creep is invisible until the invoice arrives.

section.title: Vendor Sourcing
section.p: Use the right sourcing instrument: RFI to gather market info, RFP for formal solution proposals, RFQ for price quotes on defined work, sole-source only when justified with documented rationale.

rule.statement: Require references AND a structured reference-call rubric. References without a rubric are positive-bias noise.
rule.rationale: Vendors select their best references; a structured interview reveals actual weaknesses.

rule.statement: Track deliverables against SOW, reconcile invoices against milestones, and maintain a quarterly vendor scorecard (on-time %, defect rate, escalation count). Renew or churn based on data.
rule.rationale: Set-and-forget vendor management is the second most common procurement failure mode after vague SOWs.

rule.statement: Negotiate exit terms before signing: notice period, data handback, transition assistance, IP transfer. Trapped-buyer is the most common contract failure mode.
rule.rationale: Renegotiating exit terms after the relationship sours produces the worst outcomes for both parties.
